impacts.statement: Severe damage occurs in Pittsburgh with up to 15 feet of water in the golden triangle. This is the level of the record flood in march of 1936.
PNC Park is flooded along Federal Street.
Water reaches the floor of Brunots Island Power Station.
Water reaches the floor level of Rivers Casino.
Steam distribution lines in Pittsburgh are in jeopardy. Water reaches the top of the flood wall at PNC park.
Serious flood damage occurs to about 500 businesses on the North Side and in downtown Pittsburgh. The Port Authority of Pittsburgh subway system is affected.
Flood waters reach the historic levels of Hurricane Agnes on June 26, 1972.
The river level reaches a critical stage for Acrisure Stadium and PNC Park.
Mckees Rocks is affected. Flooding occurs on Neville Island.
Water reaches the railroad tracks at Station Square on the South Side. PNC Park playing field is flooded. Flood waters reach the level of Hurricane Ivan on September 18, 2004.
200 manufacturing concerns and companies in Pittsburgh are affected by flooding. Acrisure Stadium playing field is flooded. Point state park is flooded to the Portal Bridge.
River Avenue on the North Side begins to flood.
Pumping operations begin on Neville Island. The Wood Street T station facilities are impacted. Fort Pitt Boulevard is closed.
The fountain in Point State Park is completely submerged.
The Parkway Central (also known as the bathtub) is closed by flooding. Lower North Side businesses are affected.
Water enters the inner pool of the fountain.
Water enters the outer pool of the fountain.
The fountain plaza is flooded.
The Tenth Street Bypass floods.
Water is up to one foot deep in the lower areas of the North Shore Riverwalk.
The Monongahela Parking Wharf is completely flooded.
Water begins to flow onto the North Shore Riverwalk between the stadiums.
Water begins to flow onto the Monongahela Parking Wharf.

dataAttribution.title: Observations courtesy of U.S. Geological Survey

hydronotes.statement: The above river forecast includes future rainfall/snowmelt. Forecast stages may be different if the actual rainfall/snowmelt is different than the forecast rainfall/snowmelt.

value.label: North American Vertical Datum of 1988
value.description: A fixed reference for elevations determined by geodetic leveling. The datum was derived from a general adjustment of the first-order terrestrial leveling nets of the United States, Canada, and Mexico. In the adjustment, only the height of the primary tidal bench mark, referenced to the International Great Lakes Datum of 1985 (IGLD 85) local mean sea level height value, at Father Point, Rimouski, Quebec, Canada was held fixed, thus providing minimum constraint. NAVD 88 and IGLD 85 are identical. However, NAVD 88 bench mark values are given in Helmert orthometric height units while IGLD 85 values are in dynamic heights. See International Great Lakes Datum of 1985, National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929, and geopotential difference. NAVD 88 should not be used as Mean Sea Level.
